faq.question: What is the MiCBT Guide app?
faq.answer: The MiCBT Guide is a mobile app that guides you step-by-step through the full Mindfulness-integrated Cognitive Behaviour Therapy (MiCBT) program, developed by Dr Bruno Cayoun. The app delivers a structured 4-stage, 10-week program combining body-based mindfulness meditation with evidence-based CBT techniques. The app includes: Guided audio meditations from Dr Bruno CayounVideo explanations for each stageSeven validated assessment tools to track your progressA practice diaryGoal tracking The app is available on iOS and Android.

faq.question: How do I create an account?
faq.answer: Open the app and tap Create Account on the welcome screen.Enter your first name, last name, and email address.Choose a password and tap Sign Up. Alternatively, tap Continue with Google, Continue with Facebook, or Continue with Apple to sign up using one of those accounts. Once your account is created, you can log in on any device and your data will be saved to your account.

faq.question: How do I log in to the app?
faq.answer: Open the app and tap Log in on the welcome screen.Enter your email address and password, then tap Log in. Or tap Continue with Google, Continue with Facebook, or Continue with Apple to use one of those accounts. If you have forgotten your password, tap Forgot Password on the login screen, enter your email address, and follow the reset link sent to your inbox. Check your spam folder if the email doesn't arrive within a few minutes.

faq.question: How do I reset my password?
faq.answer: On the login screen, tap Forgot Password.Enter the email address you registered with and tap Reset Password.Check your inbox for a reset email — if it doesn't arrive within a few minutes, check your spam folder.Tap the link in the email, choose a new password, and log back in. Note: this option is only available if you registered with an email address and password. If you signed up with Google, Facebook, or Apple, use those services to manage your login.

faq.question: How do I get started with the program?
faq.answer: Tap Home in the bottom navigation bar.Tap Stage 1 (the circled number 1).Tap Step 1 — Committing to Self-Care, then tap Start.Set your goals — describe what you would like to work on and rate how much each difficulty affects your life.Read through the stage guide.Begin your first meditation using the audio provided.After completing a session, log it in your Practice Diary. The program runs for 10 weeks across four stages. Work through each step in order — later stages build directly on earlier skills.

faq.question: Should I unlock all stages or work through them in order?
faq.answer: We recommend keeping the stages locked and working through them in sequence. MiCBT is a progressive program — each stage builds on the skills developed in the one before it. Jumping ahead can undermine the effectiveness of the training, because later practices assume you have developed the foundational skills from earlier ones. If you are curious about upcoming content, tap More and toggle Unlock All Stages to preview it — but continue working through your current stage for your actual practice.

faq.question: How does the program work? What is the overall structure?
faq.answer: The MiCBT Guide delivers a 10-week, 4-stage program: Stage 1 — Personal Stage (Weeks 1–4): Develops foundational mindfulness skills — breath awareness, body scanning, and emotional regulation through equanimity.Stage 2 — Interpersonal Awareness Stage (Weeks 5–6): Applies skills to overcome avoidance and build self-confidence using Bipolar Exposure.Stage 3 — Interpersonal Stage (Weeks 7–8): Focuses on improving relationships through mindful communication and the assertiveness technique of Experiential Ownership.Stage 4 — Compassion Stage (Weeks 9–10): Cultivates compassion for self and others through loving-kindness meditation and ethical living. Each stage includes guided meditations (twice daily), assessments, video guidance, readings, and a practice diary.

faq.question: How do I navigate the app?
faq.answer: The app has five main sections in the bottom navigation bar: Home — your current stage and stepDiary — your practice logGoals — set and review your goalsAssessments — all seven tracking toolsMore — settings, timers, reminders, and other options From Home, tap your current stage number, then the step you are working on. Each step contains a guide, audio instructions, and links to relevant assessments.

faq.question: How do I log my meditation practice?
faq.answer: Option 1 — from Home: Tap Home from the main menu.Select the relevant stage and step number you are currently on.Tap Log my practice and enter the details.Tap Submit to save. Option 2 — from Diary: Tap Diary in the bottom navigation bar.Tap Display Log.Tap the + icon.Enter your practice details and tap Submit.

faq.question: How do I view my practice history?
faq.answer: Tap Diary in the bottom navigation bar.Tap Display Log (orange button). Your log shows all recorded sessions. Switch between Week, Month, and Year views using the tabs at the top. The colour-coded calendar gives you a quick visual of your consistency.

faq.question: How do I set a meditation reminder?
faq.answer: Tap More and select My Reminders.Tap the + icon to add a new reminder.Choose the time of day.Select the day(s) of the week.Tap Add Reminder to save. You can set multiple reminders — for example, one for a morning session and one for an evening session. To edit a reminder, select it, make your changes, and tap Modify Reminder. To delete, swipe left and tap the trash icon.

faq.question: How do I create a meditation timer?
faq.answer: Tap More > My Timers.Tap the + icon.Enter a name for your timer.Use the slider to set the duration.Tap Save. To edit a timer, swipe right and tap the pen icon. To delete, swipe left and tap the trash icon.

faq.question: Can I look ahead to see what's in upcoming stages?
faq.answer: Yes. Tap More, then toggle the switch next to Unlock All Stages. This gives you access to all steps and stages in the app. This is useful for previewing content, but we recommend continuing to work through your current stage in sequence for your actual practice — the program is designed to build skills progressively.

faq.question: How do I avoid being interrupted by phone calls or notifications during meditation?
faq.answer: We recommend turning on Do Not Disturb mode before each session. On iPhone: Swipe down from the top right of your display to open Control Center.Long-tap the Focus or moon icon.Select Do Not Disturb. Alternatively, go to Settings > Focus > Do Not Disturb. On Android: Swipe down from the top of your screen to open Quick Settings.Tap Do Not Disturb. Remember to turn Do Not Disturb off again after your session.

faq.question: How do I rewind or fast-forward the meditation audio?
faq.answer: Tap the fast-forward or rewind button on the audio player. Each tap moves the time by 5 seconds. Note: the slider bar displays your position in the audio but does not support dragging to skip — use the tap buttons instead.

faq.question: What is MiCBT?
faq.answer: MiCBT (Mindfulness-integrated Cognitive Behaviour Therapy) was developed by Dr Bruno Cayoun. It combines two evidence-based approaches: mindfulness meditation and cognitive behaviour therapy (CBT). Traditional CBT helps you identify and change unhelpful thoughts and behaviours. Mindfulness meditation develops your ability to observe thoughts and body sensations without automatically reacting to them. MiCBT integrates these by using body-based mindfulness — particularly body scanning — to address the physical dimension of emotional experience. The program has been used with thousands of people across many countries, ages, and cultures, including those with anxiety, depression, PTSD, chronic pain, and everyday stress.

faq.question: Who developed MiCBT and who delivers the guided meditations?
faq.answer: Dr Bruno Cayoun is the developer of MiCBT and the meditation instructor in the app. He is a clinical psychologist and researcher who developed MiCBT over more than two decades of clinical practice and research. The meditations are delivered in his voice — he has a French accent, which some find charming! The current book underpinning the program is Mindfulness-integrated CBT for Well-Being and Personal Growth (Second Edition, 2026), co-authored by Dr Cayoun and Dr Andrea Grabovac.

faq.question: What is equanimity and why does it matter?
faq.answer: Equanimity is the ability to observe your experience — particularly body sensations — without reacting to it. Not suppressing it, not amplifying it. Just noticing, with stable, open awareness. In everyday life, we automatically react to what we feel. Pleasant sensations draw us in — we want more. Unpleasant ones push us away — we want them gone. These automatic reactions (craving and aversion) drive much of our distress, avoidance, and unhelpful behaviour. MiCBT trains equanimity through body scanning: you practise noticing sensations — whatever they are — without reacting. Over time, this changes how you relate to your experience across all areas of life. Research by Dr Alice Shires, Dr Cayoun, and colleagues shows equanimity is the core mechanism of benefit in MiCBT.

faq.question: What is the Co-Emergence Model of Reinforcement (CMR)?
faq.answer: The Co-Emergence Model of Reinforcement (CMR) is the theoretical framework developed by Dr Cayoun to explain how thoughts and body sensations interact to create and maintain habits and emotional patterns. The key idea: whenever a thought arises, a corresponding body sensation arises with it — and vice versa. This is co-emergence. When we react to the body sensation (by trying to hold on to pleasant ones or push away unpleasant ones), we reinforce the associated thought pattern. This is how emotional habits get strengthened over time. MiCBT uses body scanning to interrupt this cycle: by observing sensations with equanimity instead of reacting to them, we stop reinforcing the unhelpful thought-sensation loop, and over time the patterns weaken.

faq.question: What is body scanning and why do we practise it?
faq.answer: Body scanning is a meditation practice where you systematically move your attention through the body, part by part, noticing whatever sensations are present — without trying to change them. In MiCBT, body scanning is the central practice because emotions are experienced in the body (as sensations) before — and alongside — the thoughts we have about them. By practising equanimity toward body sensations, you develop the ability to experience difficult emotions without being overwhelmed or driven to react. Over the 10-week program, scanning evolves progressively: Weeks 3–4: Part-by-part awarenessWeek 5: Symmetrical bilateral scanningWeek 6: Sweeping en masseWeek 7: Continued sweeping + exposureWeek 8: Transversal scanningWeeks 9–10: Sweeping in depth

faq.question: Why does MiCBT work in stages?
faq.answer: The four stages build on each other deliberately: Stage 1 (Personal): Develops sustained attention and equanimity toward your own internal experience.Stage 2 (Interpersonal Awareness): Applies those skills outward — to situations you have been avoiding and to building self-confidence.Stage 3 (Interpersonal): Extends mindfulness into relationships — how you relate to and communicate with others.Stage 4 (Compassion): Cultivates generosity toward self and others, and establishes ethical living as a foundation for lasting wellbeing. Each stage assumes the skills of the previous one. Practising Stage 2 exposure work without the equanimity built in Stage 1, for example, simply means bringing more reactivity to feared situations rather than less.

faq.question: What is the difference between MiCBT and traditional mindfulness programs like MBSR or MBCT?
faq.answer: While all mindfulness programs share the goal of developing present-moment awareness, MiCBT has several distinctive features: Body sensations at the centre: The Co-Emergence Model explains that emotional habits are maintained through our reaction to body sensations, so equanimity toward sensations is the core mechanism of change.Explicit integration with CBT: MiCBT combines body-based mindfulness with structured CBT components — graded exposure (SUDS hierarchy), Bipolar Exposure, and assertiveness training.Four-stage scope: The program addresses personal wellbeing, behaviour, relationships, and compassion — not just symptom reduction.Purpose-built assessment tools: The MISS, IAI, and ES-16 are specifically designed to measure the mechanisms the program targets.

faq.question: Who can benefit from MiCBT?
faq.answer: MiCBT can benefit anyone seeking to improve their mental and emotional wellbeing, whether or not they have a diagnosed condition. The program has demonstrated benefits for people across countries and cultures, and for ages from about seven upward. It is particularly helpful for: Anxiety and stressDepressionPTSD and traumaChronic painRelationship difficultiesLow self-confidenceAnyone seeking greater clarity, calm, and emotional resilience People using it alongside therapy often find the two approaches complement each other well. For those with severe trauma or a current mental health crisis, working with a trained MiCBT facilitator alongside the app is recommended.

faq.question: Is being really focused on a task the same as mindfulness?
faq.answer: Not quite. While mindfulness always involves attentiveness, not all attentiveness is mindfulness. For example, a surgeon concentrating on an operation, or an athlete in 'the zone', is highly focused — but this is single-pointed, goal-directed attention. Mindfulness involves a broader, open, non-reactive awareness: you are aware of what you are experiencing (thoughts, sensations, emotions) without being caught up in or identified with those experiences. You can be fully absorbed in a task and still be mindful — but the mindfulness is in the quality of awareness, not just the intensity of concentration.

faq.question: What do I practise in Stage 1.1 (Week 1)?
faq.answer: Practice: Mindfulness of breath — 20 minutes, twice daily. Stage 1.1 establishes the foundations: a regular practice schedule, a clear commitment to yourself, and your first experiences of formal mindfulness meditation. The core practice is breath awareness: sit comfortably, close your eyes, and rest your attention on the natural sensations of breathing — the touch of air at the nostrils, or the rise and fall of the chest or abdomen. When the mind wanders, notice that it has wandered and gently return attention to the breath, without judgment. You also begin Progressive Muscle Relaxation (PMR) this stage: systematically tensing and releasing muscle groups to develop body awareness and the contrast between tension and relaxation. Set your goals at the start of this stage so you have a clear sense of what you are working toward.

faq.question: What do I practise in Stage 1.2 (Week 2)?
faq.answer: Practice: Mindfulness of breath — 30 minutes, twice daily. Stage 1.2 extends breath awareness to 30 minutes and deepens attention regulation. With more time in practice, you will notice more of what the mind does — the stories it tells, the places it wanders. The key skill being developed is recognising when attention has moved away from the breath and returning it — again and again, without frustration. Each return is the practice. This week also introduces mindfulness of thoughts: noticing thoughts as mental events rather than facts, and not getting entangled in them. Keep your Practice Diary consistently from this stage.

faq.question: What do I practise in Stage 1.3 (Week 3)?
faq.answer: Practice: Body scanning — 30 minutes, twice daily. Stage 1.3 introduces the body scan — the central practice of MiCBT. Instead of resting attention on the breath, you move it systematically through the body, part by part, noticing whatever sensations are present: tingling, pressure, temperature, vibration, pain, numbness, or nothing at all. The instruction is to observe with equanimity — not to try to change or fix sensations, but simply to notice them. This is where the Co-Emergence Model becomes tangible: you begin to see how emotions arise as body sensations, and practise not reacting to them. It is normal for some areas to feel blank (no sensation) — interoceptive awareness develops gradually with consistent practice.

faq.question: What do I practise in Stage 1.4 (Week 4)?
faq.answer: Practice: Body scanning + mindful activities. Stage 1.4 begins the transfer of mindfulness skills into daily life. Alongside your formal body scanning sessions, you bring mindful awareness into everyday activities — eating, walking, washing dishes, conversations. This is not about doing things slowly or reverently. It is about noticing what is actually happening in each moment rather than moving through life on autopilot. Immobility practice is also introduced: remaining still during body scanning even when there is an urge to move, scratch, or adjust. This develops the capacity to tolerate discomfort without reacting — a skill that transfers directly to difficult emotions. Review your goals this stage — several weeks in, they may need updating.

faq.question: What do I practise in Stage 2.1 (Week 5)?
faq.answer: Practice: Bilateral (symmetrical) body scanning + SUDS hierarchy work. Stage 2.1 introduces bilateral scanning — attention moves simultaneously across both sides of the body (left and right together). You also begin structured exposure work using the SUDS hierarchy: identifying five situations you typically avoid and beginning to face them in order of increasing difficulty. The body scanning develops the equanimity needed to face avoided situations without being overwhelmed.

faq.question: What do I practise in Stage 2.2 (Week 6)?
faq.answer: Practice: Bilateral scanning + Bipolar Exposure. Stage 2.2 focuses on self-confidence using Bipolar Exposure: a two-step imagination technique where you first vividly imagine a worst-case scenario in a feared situation (while observing body sensations with equanimity) and then shift to a best-case scenario. By developing equanimity toward both extremes, you reduce the emotional charge of feared outcomes and increase resilience. Sweeping en masse is introduced in the formal meditation: attention moves through the body in a broad, flowing wave rather than part by part.

faq.question: What do I practise in Stage 3.1 (Week 7)?
faq.answer: Practice: Bilateral scanning + interpersonal awareness. Stage 3.1 extends mindfulness into relationships. The focus is on interpersonal awareness — noticing how you respond in social situations, what arises in the body when you are with others, and how habitual patterns play out in interactions. Experiential Ownership (a seven-statement assertiveness framework) is introduced, helping you communicate needs and boundaries clearly and respectfully while remaining mindful of body sensations.

faq.question: What do I practise in Stage 3.2 (Week 8)?
faq.answer: Practice: Bilateral scanning + mindful speech. Stage 3.2 focuses on mindful communication — how you speak, listen, and engage with others. Mindful speech practice involves noticing body sensations before and during communication: what arises when you want to say something difficult, when you feel unheard, or when a conversation goes well. Transversal scanning is introduced in the formal meditation: attention moves through the body in a front-to-back direction, developing even finer interoceptive awareness.

faq.question: What do I practise in Stage 4.1 (Week 9)?
faq.answer: Practice: Loving-kindness (metta) meditation + bilateral scanning + ECD. Stage 4.1 introduces loving-kindness meditation — deliberately cultivating goodwill toward yourself and others. After three stages of inward work, this stage turns attention outward. The practice begins with yourself (the hardest person for many), then extends to loved ones, neutral people, difficult people, and ultimately all beings. Loving-kindness can feel forced at first — that reaction is itself worth noticing and observing with equanimity. The ECD (Ethical Challenges Diary) is also introduced this stage.

faq.question: What do I practise in Stage 4.2 (Week 10)?
faq.answer: Practice: Full integration — sweeping in depth + relapse prevention. Stage 4.2 is the culmination of the program. All practices come together: breath awareness, body scanning, bilateral scanning, loving-kindness, the assessments, and the ECD. Sweeping in depth is introduced — the final and most refined scanning technique, where attention penetrates simultaneously through all layers of the body. This can produce strong sensations of bliss or unexpected emotions — both are normal and should be observed with equanimity. The focus of Stage 4.2 is sustainability: reviewing your goals, comparing your final assessment scores to your Stage 1.1 baselines, and establishing a practice that continues beyond the program.

faq.question: I'm struggling to make time for practice. What if I don't have time?
faq.answer: Starting small is better than not starting at all. Even one daily session is more beneficial than none, though research shows two daily sessions produce significantly better results. The key is consistency, not duration. Most people find that once they experience the genuine benefits of practice, they naturally make time for it. Try setting a specific time each day — perhaps right after waking or before bed — and treat it like an appointment with yourself. After a couple of weeks of consistent practice, it typically becomes a natural habit.

faq.question: Why do I need to practise twice daily? Can I just do it once?
faq.answer: Once daily is better than not practising at all. However, research shows that twice-daily practice produces significantly better results. The schedule is deliberate: the morning session refreshes the skills developed the evening before, and the evening session consolidates what happened during the day. If twice daily feels genuinely impossible right now, prioritise once daily and work toward adding the second session when circumstances allow.

faq.question: I keep falling asleep during meditation. What should I do?
faq.answer: Sleepiness during meditation (known as torpor) is one of the classic challenges of practice, especially in the early weeks or during evening sessions. Several things help: Sit upright with your spine self-supporting — not leaning against a wall or chair back.Keep your eyes slightly open, directed downward at a 45-degree angle rather than fully closed.Choose a slightly cooler environment.Avoid practising immediately after a heavy meal or when sleep-deprived.If evening sleepiness is a consistent problem, try moving your evening session earlier in the evening. Drowsiness often signals that your nervous system is genuinely relaxing — not always bad — but if it consistently prevents engagement with the practice, the adjustments above should help.

faq.question: Can I lie down during meditation?
faq.answer: For maintenance practice once the program is complete, yes. However, during the initial 10-week program, practising in an upright seated position is strongly recommended for two reasons: Lying down — especially when tired — makes falling asleep much more likely, which prevents skill development.The upright posture trains your nervous system to associate that position with focused, alert attention. If sitting fully upright is difficult due to back or other physical issues, sitting in a chair with back support is a good alternative.

faq.question: My mind keeps wandering — what should I do?
faq.answer: A wandering mind is one of the most common experiences in meditation — and it is not a problem. It is the practice. The instruction is not to keep a blank mind. The instruction is to notice when attention has wandered and return it to the object of meditation (the breath, the body sensation). Every time you notice the wandering and return, you have practised. That moment of recognition is itself the key skill being developed. Over time, the gaps between wandering become longer and the return becomes quicker — but this develops gradually through consistent practice, including the sessions that feel difficult.

faq.question: I have intrusive thoughts during meditation. Does this mean my mind is too busy?
faq.answer: No. Intrusive thoughts are universal — the goal of mindfulness is not to stop thinking but to change your relationship with thoughts. Notice them without engaging, like clouds passing through the sky. Over time, as you practise equanimity, you react less to thoughts and they naturally have less power over you. Intrusive thoughts during meditation often simply reflect what is stored in your memory — they are not problems to be solved, they are objects of observation.

faq.question: Should I try to make my mind blank during meditation?
faq.answer: No. A blank mind is neither the goal nor realistic. The goal is to develop equanimity toward thoughts and sensations — noticing them without identification or reaction. If you expect blankness and judge yourself for 'failing,' you are creating suffering. Accept the mind as it is — active, busy, sometimes chaotic — and practise observing it without being swept away.

faq.question: I get caught up in rumination during practice — memories and worries keep taking over. What do I do?
faq.answer: In MiCBT, rather than simply redirecting away from the thought, we bring attention to the body sensations that co-emerge with it. For example, if worry arises, notice where you feel it in the body — tightness in the chest, a knot in the stomach — and observe those sensations with equanimity, without the accompanying story. This directly addresses the physical dimension of rumination rather than just redirecting away from it. Over time, this weakens the habit of rumination at its root.

faq.question: I experience physical pain during meditation. Should I stop?
faq.answer: Physical discomfort or pain during meditation is common and rarely dangerous. Mindfulness practice doesn't cause pain — it reveals what is already there (just as washing dirty clothes doesn't create dirt; it brings out what was already present). If the pain is clearly posture-related, adjust your position.If the pain is manageable, observe it with equanimity — research suggests that focusing on an area of discomfort with equanimity can significantly reduce the intensity of unpleasantness within 30 seconds.If the pain is severe or you are concerned about a medical issue, consult your doctor.

faq.question: I have a history of trauma and sometimes get flashbacks during meditation. Is this normal? What should I do?
faq.answer: Flashbacks or trauma-related memories arising during meditation can happen for those with a history of trauma. Increased body awareness can sometimes bring previously suppressed memories to the surface. If you experience a flashback: Pause the session if needed.Ground yourself — notice five things you can see, four you can hear, three you can touch.Return to normal breathing.Do not force yourself to continue if the distress is significant. For those with significant trauma history, working with a trained MiCBT facilitator or therapist alongside the app is strongly recommended. For manageable flashbacks, gently observing the associated body sensations with equanimity — rather than trying to suppress them — is the MiCBT approach to healing.

faq.question: I experience strong emotions or emotional releases during body scanning. Is something wrong?
faq.answer: No — strong sensations and emotions during body scanning are normal, not a sign that something has gone wrong. When you bring sustained, equanimous attention to an area of the body, sensations that were there all along become more noticeable. What feels overwhelming may simply be awareness catching up with what was already present. When this happens: Briefly return attention to the breath as an anchor to stabilise yourself.Remind yourself this is the practice working, not failing.When you feel slightly more settled, gently return to the body scan. If the overwhelm is persistent or significantly affects your daily life, consider slowing down (returning to an earlier stage temporarily) or seeking support from a therapist or trained MiCBT facilitator.

faq.question: I feel nothing during body scanning. Is something wrong?
faq.answer: No — feeling very little in early body scanning is completely normal. Interoceptive sensitivity is a skill that develops through consistent practice, not an innate ability. If you are not feeling much: Scan slowly and give each area a moment of attention before moving on.Try not to visualise or imagine the body part — the instruction is to feel, not see.Check you are not tensing other muscles while trying to feel.Use the IAI regularly to track your awareness across the body over weeks — you will almost certainly notice improvement. Some people develop rich sensitivity within days; for others it takes several weeks. Both are fine.

faq.question: I feel agitated or restless during meditation. Is this normal?
faq.answer: Yes — agitation and restlessness are common, especially at the beginning of practice. Rather than a problem, this may be valuable information: agitation often points to stored tension or emotional energy that is now becoming perceptible through the practice. Observe the quality and location of the restlessness with equanimity. Don't try to suppress it or force calmness — simply notice it. Over time, as equanimity develops, agitation during practice typically settles.

faq.question: During meditation, my eyes move toward the area I'm scanning. Is that a problem?
faq.answer: This is a common experience when starting body scanning. Many people instinctively use their visual sense to 'support' the movement of attention. However, this creates unnecessary tension in the eye muscles and can cause headaches or tiredness. The practice is to move attention internally, without visual support. Try letting your gaze remain soft and still — eyes closed, or gently downcast — while moving your felt sense of attention through the body. With practice, the habit of using the eyes diminishes.

faq.question: I practise PMR but I dislike the feeling of tightening my muscles. Do I have to do it?
faq.answer: PMR uses the contrast between tension and relaxation to help you recognise what relaxation actually feels like — this awareness is foundational for the later body scanning practices. If the tensing is unpleasant, you can reduce the intensity — tense to about 50% of your maximum rather than full tension. This still creates enough contrast to develop the skill. If you have a physical condition that makes tensing painful (such as back injury or inflammatory conditions), simply omit the tense phase for those areas and focus on the releasing phase only.

faq.question: I feel anxious during PMR. Am I doing something wrong?
faq.answer: No. It is common to feel unsettled during the first few PMR sessions. PMR itself does not create anxiety — rather, the increased body awareness can bring already-present tension or anxiety to conscious attention. The anxiety you feel is likely what has been there all along, now becoming noticeable through the practice. Continue practising calmly with the sensations rather than trying to push them away. This is equanimity in action. If the anxiety is severe or distressing, pause and speak with a healthcare provider.

faq.question: I have back or health issues. How do I adapt the PMR practice?
faq.answer: Be gentle with areas that need special care. When instructions suggest arching or tensing your back, adapt the movement or skip that area entirely and focus on the releasing phase only. Similarly, if you have joint pain or other physical conditions, modify any movement that causes sharp pain. The general principle: if an area is contraindicated, focus your attention on that area during the release phase only, noticing any sensations present without adding tension. Your safety always comes first.

faq.question: I experienced an out-of-body experience during meditation. Is that okay?
faq.answer: Unusual experiences — feeling like you are floating, observing yourself from above, or an intense sense of dissolution — are not uncommon for meditators who reach certain levels of skill. The instruction is to observe these experiences with equanimity rather than becoming attached to or frightened by them. If you find yourself enjoying or pursuing the experience, notice the craving that arises; if frightened, notice the aversion. These responses are themselves opportunities to practise equanimity. Such experiences, while interesting, are not the goal of meditation. The goal is the continued development of equanimity.

faq.question: Why do I have obsessive thoughts or strong cravings (e.g., for food, sex, or other things) during practice?
faq.answer: Cravings and obsessive thoughts during body scanning are common and are explained by the Co-Emergence Model: when sensations arise in the body, they can trigger associated memories and desires — and the reverse is true too. The practice is to observe these experiences — both the thought and the co-emerging sensation — with equanimity, without engaging in the story or pursuing the experience. A useful question to ask: could it be that what you are actually craving is the body sensation itself, rather than the object of desire?

faq.question: I have found that mindfulness of breath calms my mind. Why do I also need to do body scanning?
faq.answer: Mindfulness of breath is an excellent tool for calming and stabilising the mind, and you can always return to it as a reliable refuge. However, it primarily develops concentration and attentional stability. Body scanning does something additional: it develops equanimity toward the physical dimension of emotional experience. Most of our emotional suffering is maintained through our reaction to body sensations — the tightness in the chest, the knot in the stomach, the heat of anger. Body scanning trains you to notice these directly and observe them without reacting, addressing the root mechanism of emotional reactivity rather than simply calming the surface.

faq.question: Why do I scan the body in a specific order (top to bottom)?
faq.answer: There are three reasons for the consistent top-to-bottom order: Completeness: Scanning in the same order ensures you don't skip any areas, especially smaller or less obvious ones.Sustained attention: Moving attention in a predictable, systematic direction trains the kind of sustained, methodical attention that transfers to how you engage with challenges in daily life.Research integrity: Scanning in the standard order maintains the integrity of the practice as taught and researched.

faq.question: How fast should I scan during body scanning?
faq.answer: During the part-by-part body scan in the early weeks, the audio instructions guide you through one full body cycle in your 30-minute session. A body cycle means scanning from the top of the head down to the toes. The pace is deliberately unhurried — spend a few seconds noticing any sensation in each area before moving on. If you complete a full cycle before the session ends, simply begin again from the top. The goal is sensitivity and equanimity, not speed.

faq.question: I can't feel sensations in certain parts of my body. What should I do?
faq.answer: This is entirely normal, especially when starting body scanning. When you reach a blank area: Spend a brief moment of attention there, then move on — don't dwell.Make sure you are not trying to visualise the body part — the instruction is to feel, not see.Avoid assuming nothing is there — very subtle sensations (a faint warmth, a slight tingling) may be present and only become perceptible with patience.Track your progress with the IAI tool in Assessments — you will see improvement over weeks.

faq.question: How fast should I scan with symmetrical (bilateral) scanning?
faq.answer: Move at your own pace. When you encounter blank areas — places where sensations are not noticeable — don't interrupt your flow. Continue scanning as if the sensations are there, even if you can't feel them yet. They will develop with practice. There is no perfect speed. The aim is to maintain steady, unhurried attention across both sides of the body simultaneously. The audio instructions will guide you through the technique.

faq.question: I can't feel some body parts symmetrically. What should I do?
faq.answer: This is very common — don't expect to feel both sides perfectly symmetrically at first. There will often be a 'lead-lag' between sides, or one side may feel richer in sensation than the other. This is normal and typically reflects how your nervous system has learned to represent different parts of the body. Continue scanning both sides together, even if the experience is not symmetrical. Over days and weeks of practice, the bilateral sensitivity tends to even out.

faq.question: How deep should I feel when sweeping? Just the surface, or deeper inside the body?
faq.answer: At the sweeping stage, don't worry about how deep you are feeling. It is perfectly fine to only feel sensations on the surface of the skin. If you naturally begin to notice sensations slightly deeper — a warmth or pressure inside a limb — allow that, but don't force it. Depth of awareness develops naturally as the practice matures. Trying to force deeper sensation creates unnecessary effort and tension, which is counterproductive. Simply allow your attention to rest wherever sensations are present.

faq.question: During sweeping, I experience tingling, energy flowing, or dissolution of body parts. Is this real?
faq.answer: Yes — these are real perceptual experiences, not imagination. As your interoceptive awareness deepens, you become aware of increasingly subtle sensations throughout the body. The tingling or flowing sensation is commonly reported at the sweeping stage. These experiences, while fascinating, should be observed with equanimity rather than attachment — they are phenomena to be noticed, not goals to be chased. If you find yourself trying to recreate them, notice the craving that arises and observe that with equanimity too.

faq.question: When sweeping en masse, what do I do when sweeping doesn't happen in some areas?
faq.answer: When you notice that sweeping seems to bypass certain areas, return to part-by-part scanning for those specific areas until sensations become more apparent, then resume sweeping. You do not need to scan the whole body part by part — only the areas where sweeping isn't occurring. This combined approach — alternating sweeping and targeted part-by-part scanning — ensures you don't leave entire regions unattended.

faq.question: During sweeping en masse, do I need to return to full part-by-part scanning?
faq.answer: No — only for the areas where sweeping isn't happening. When you go back to part-by-part scanning after two or three sweepings, focus only on the specific areas where you did not feel sensations as readily during the sweep. Once those areas show up more clearly, return to sweeping. You do not need to restart from the top of the head and scan every area.

faq.question: What is transversal scanning and why is it introduced?
faq.answer: Transversal scanning (introduced in Stage 3.2 / Week 8) is a technique where attention moves through the body in a front-to-back direction (or back-to-front), rather than top-to-bottom or side-to-side. Words in the audio instructions like 'piercingly' or 'going through the body' describe the quality of attention required — they do not describe a physical process. What is actually happening is that you are activating neural pathways in the somatosensory and insular cortices, which represent sensations from different parts of the body. Transversal scanning develops a qualitatively different, finer, and more penetrating interoceptive awareness that builds on what sweeping has developed.

faq.question: Transversal scanning feels harder than sweeping. Do I really have to use it?
faq.answer: Yes — transversal scanning is an important part of the program's development, even though it can initially feel more laborious than sweeping. It is natural to prefer sweeping because it often feels pleasant as attention flows freely through the body. The fact that you miss it is worth examining: have you become attached to the pleasant sensations of sweeping? Transversal scanning is actually an excellent opportunity to observe this attachment — and to practise equanimity toward the loss of something pleasant. The technique develops a qualitatively different kind of body awareness — finer and more penetrating — that builds on and deepens what sweeping has developed.

faq.question: I sometimes feel very blissful during meditation. Is this good? Should I try to recreate it?
faq.answer: Blissful states are a natural outcome of deepening practice and equanimity — they are welcome. But like all experiences in meditation, they are impermanent. If you notice yourself craving or trying to recreate a blissful experience, you are practising attachment — the opposite of equanimity. After an unusually blissful session, some people feel flat or disappointed the next day: this is precisely the consequence of having attached to the pleasant experience. The instruction is to observe bliss with the same equanimity you bring to discomfort: appreciate it, but don't cling to it.

faq.question: What is sweeping in depth and what is it like?
faq.answer: Sweeping in depth is the final and most refined scanning technique, introduced in Stage 4.2 (Week 10). Rather than moving through the body in a particular direction, attention penetrates simultaneously through all layers — skin, muscle, bone, organs — throughout the entire body. This can produce: An intense sense of spaciousness or blissApparent dissolution of the body into sensationUnexpected emotions — sadness, grief, unexpected warmth All of these are normal. Observe everything with equanimity. This technique completes the journey from gross awareness of obvious sensations to the subtlest levels of body experience.

faq.question: What is Bipolar Exposure and how does it work?
faq.answer: Bipolar Exposure is a two-step imagination technique used in Stage 2.2 to build self-confidence and reduce the emotional charge of feared situations. Step 1 — Worst case: Vividly imagine a feared situation going as badly as possible, while observing the body sensations that arise with equanimity — without trying to suppress the distress. Hold this for several minutes. Step 2 — Best case: Shift to imagining the same situation going as well as possible. Observe the body sensations equanimously — notice the pleasant sensations without clinging to them. By practising equanimity at both extremes, you reduce the overall emotional reactivity you have toward the situation. This makes it easier to face the situation in real life. Bipolar Exposure works best when used for a few days before facing the real situation.

faq.question: The imagined scenario during Bipolar Exposure doesn't feel real or emotionally charged. Is this a problem?
faq.answer: This can happen for a few reasons: You may have already developed significant equanimity toward that situation through earlier practice — in which case, the reduced emotional response is a sign of progress, not a problem.The scenario may not be sufficiently vivid or specific. Try making it more detailed — the setting, the people, what they say, what you feel in your body. If the emotional charge is genuinely absent, the Bipolar Exposure for that situation may have already done its work, and you can move to the next situation on your SUDS hierarchy.

faq.question: Why shouldn't I enjoy the pleasant sensations in the best-case scenario of Bipolar Exposure?
faq.answer: Enjoying pleasant situations as they arise in daily life is important for wellbeing — MiCBT does not discourage that. However, during Bipolar Exposure practice, the goal is to develop equanimity toward both extremes. If you allow yourself to become attached to the pleasant sensations of the best-case scenario during practice, you are rehearsing craving rather than equanimity. The aim is to reduce the emotional charge at both poles — not to replace one type of reactivity (anxiety) with another (craving). In real life, enjoyment is natural and encouraged; in the Bipolar Exposure practice itself, equanimity is the discipline.

faq.question: I can't think of situations I avoid. Can Bipolar Exposure still help?
faq.answer: Sometimes we don't recognise our avoidance because we have become so practised at it that we frame it differently — as 'I just don't enjoy those things' or 'I have no reason to go there.' Look for situations where your choice is driven not by genuine preference but by discomfort, anxiety, or anticipated distress. Also consider interpersonal avoidance: do you avoid certain conversations, confrontations, or people? Do you avoid expressing your real opinions or needs? If avoidance is genuinely not a feature of your difficulties, focus on the aspects of Stage 2 that address your actual patterns — the SUDS hierarchy can also include situations that cause distress even if you do not strictly avoid them.

faq.question: Can I use Bipolar Exposure for addictions, habits, or urges?
faq.answer: Yes. Bipolar Exposure can be adapted for any situation where craving or avoidance drives behaviour — including smoking, alcohol, food urges, or other habits. The key is to identify the specific triggers (contexts, sensations, or situations that produce the urge) and include them in the worst-case scenario. Imagine the trigger vividly, observe the urge in your body with equanimity rather than acting on it, then shift to the best case: imagine resisting successfully and how you feel. This gradually reduces the emotional charge of the trigger. Start with less intense triggers before working up to the strongest ones.

faq.question: What is Experiential Ownership and how does it work?
faq.answer: Experiential Ownership is a seven-statement assertiveness framework taught in Stage 3.1 (Week 7). It helps you communicate needs, boundaries, and concerns clearly and respectfully, while remaining mindful of your own body sensations. The seven statements guide you through: Acknowledging what you observeAcknowledging your possible error in judgmentExpressing how you feelStating what you needMaking a specific request of the other personStating what you are willing to doStating what the consequences will be if the situation does not change The framework is designed to reduce blame and defensiveness while maintaining clarity about your needs. With practice, the language becomes more natural and less formulaic.

faq.question: My partner becomes defensive when I try to be assertive. Am I doing something wrong?
faq.answer: Defensiveness from others is common when assertive communication is new — particularly if past interactions have been aggressive, passive, or unclear. It does not necessarily mean you are doing something wrong. A few things to check: Are you using a calm, even tone and watching your body language?Is the timing right — not during an argument or when either of you is stressed?Are the seven statements coming across as genuine ownership of your experience rather than veiled blame? It often takes time for the other person to adjust to a new style of communication. Practise the framework in lower-stakes situations first to build familiarity.

faq.question: I'm afraid my partner may become violent if I assert myself. What should I do?
faq.answer: Your concern is completely valid, and safety must come first. Assertiveness cannot function safely in a context of domestic violence or genuine threat of physical harm. If there is real risk of violence, please prioritise your safety by seeking support from a domestic violence service, trusted friend or family member, or mental health professional before attempting assertive communication. The MiCBT assertiveness techniques are designed for relationships where safety is not at risk.

faq.question: Can I use the assertiveness framework with my children?
faq.answer: Yes — the framework can be adapted for parenting. You will need to adjust the language to suit your child's age and understanding, but the principles of clear, respectful, non-blaming communication apply equally well in parent-child relationships. Your practice of equanimity in interactions with your children — staying calm while setting clear boundaries — is itself a powerful form of teaching.

faq.question: Self-compassion feels selfish or self-indulgent to me. How do I reconcile this?
faq.answer: Self-compassion is not self-indulgence. It is the recognition that this human being (you) deserves the same basic care and goodwill that you would offer to anyone who is suffering. When practising loving-kindness and saying 'May I be peaceful,' you are not prioritising yourself above others — you are recognising that sustainable care for others requires caring for yourself first. Research consistently shows that self-compassion is associated with less narcissism and more generosity toward others. It is also worth exploring: where did the belief come from that you don't deserve care? That belief itself may be worth meeting with compassion.

faq.question: I understand I should live more ethically, but the five ethical challenges sound moralistic. Does MiCBT tell me how to live?
faq.answer: Not at all. MiCBT does not make moral judgments or impose specific behaviours. The five ethical guidelines are framed not as moral rules but as practices that reduce harm — to yourself and others — and support the development of equanimity and wellbeing. There is nothing inherently wrong with flirting, sexual expression, or enjoying a drink. The question each challenge invites is: in what ways might this action cause suffering — for myself, or for others? The ECD tool in the app helps you track your own experience of applying effort in this direction — the insight comes from your own observation, not from being told what to do.

faq.question: What about white lies and gray areas in ethical conduct?
faq.answer: The key question MiCBT invites is not 'Did I lie?' but 'Did this action contribute to harm — for myself or for the other person?' A well-intentioned protective lie may have short-term benefits, but consider: does it create a precedent that undermines trust? Does it prevent the other person from receiving honest feedback they might benefit from? Does it create internal discomfort or dissonance in you? There are no simple rules. The practice is to notice your experience, consider the likely consequences for yourself and others, and make a conscious choice — rather than lying or telling hard truths habitually, without awareness.

faq.question: I feel guilty about harm I have caused in the past. How does MiCBT approach guilt?
faq.answer: MiCBT distinguishes between remorse and guilt: Remorse — recognising that you have caused harm and genuinely intending not to repeat it — is healthy. It shows growing awareness of causes and effects.Guilt — prolonged self-punishment — does not serve the other person or yourself. If you have genuinely harmed someone, the appropriate responses are remorse, learning, repair where possible, and moving forward. Continuing to punish yourself does not undo past harm; it simply perpetuates suffering. Meeting your own mistakes with equanimity — neither dismissing them nor dwelling in them — is the practice.

faq.question: What assessments are available in the app and what do they measure?
faq.answer: The app includes seven validated assessment tools: DASS-21 (Depression, Anxiety and Stress Scale) — measures symptom levels of depression, anxiety, and stress.ES-16 (Equanimity Scale) — measures your ability to observe experience without reacting. This is the core skill developed in MiCBT.MSES-R (Mindfulness-based Self-Efficacy Scale) — measures confidence in applying mindfulness skills across six life domains.SWLS (Satisfaction with Life Scale) — measures overall life satisfaction.IAI (Interoceptive Awareness Indicator) — a body map that tracks which areas you can feel during body scanning.MISS (Mindfulness-based Interoceptive Signature Scale) — assesses the quality and intensity of body sensations during distressing experiences.SUDS (Subjective Units of Distress Scale) — tracks your distress levels across the five avoidance situations you are working through.ECD (Ethical Conduct Diary) — tracks your effort to apply the five ethical challenges in daily life.

faq.question: How do I access the assessments?
faq.answer: Option 1 — from your current stage: Tap Home and select your current stage and step.Scroll to the bottom of the page and tap Assessments.Tap the assessment you would like to complete. Option 2 — directly: Tap Assessments in the bottom navigation bar.Select the relevant tool from the list.

faq.question: How do I delete an assessment entry?
faq.answer: Tap Assessments in the bottom navigation bar.Select the relevant assessment.Locate the entry you want to delete. (For some assessments, tap View Progress to see all entries.)Swipe left on the entry and tap the trash icon.

faq.question: How do I use the DASS-21?
faq.answer: Tap Assessments and select DASS-21.Read each statement and select how much it applied to you over the past week.Answer honestly — there are no right or wrong answers.Tap Submit. Your scores are tracked over time. To view all three graphs (depression, anxiety, stress), tap the orange arrow above the graph.

faq.question: What is the ES-16 (Equanimity Scale)?
faq.answer: The ES-16 measures equanimity — your ability to accept your experience and not react automatically to it. It has two subscales: Experiential Acceptance — allowing your experience to be present without fighting or suppressing it.Non-Reactivity — not being driven to act on the experience automatically. These are related but distinct. The total ES-16 score is the most accurate measure of equanimity as a combined quality, and is the primary metric to track across the program.

faq.question: What is the difference between Experiential Acceptance and Non-Reactivity on the ES-16?
faq.answer: Acceptance and non-reactivity are related but distinct: Acceptance means not fighting or trying to suppress your experience — you allow it to be there. Example: you feel an itch and don't try to push the sensation away.Non-reactivity means not being driven to act on the experience automatically. Example: you feel the itch but don't scratch it. You can accept a sensation without reacting to it (feel the itch, don't scratch), or not react without fully accepting (resist scratching but still wish it would go away). Full equanimity involves both — allowing the experience to be present and not being driven to react to it.

faq.question: What does the MSES-R measure?
faq.answer: The MSES-R (Mindfulness-based Self-Efficacy Scale — Revised) measures your confidence in using mindfulness skills across six life domains: Emotion Regulation — managing intense emotions without being overwhelmed.Equanimity — observing experiences without reactivity.Social Skills — engaging effectively and mindfully in social situations.Distress Tolerance — tolerating difficult experiences without avoidance.Taking Responsibility — recognising and owning the impact of your actions.Interpersonal Effectiveness — communicating in ways that reflect your values. The total score across all six subscales is the most direct measure of overall mindfulness-based self-efficacy.

faq.question: What is the IAI (Interoceptive Awareness Indicator)?
faq.answer: Interoceptive awareness is the ability to feel sensations within the body — just as vision is the ability to see. The IAI is a body map tool where you colour in all areas of the body where you can feel sensations during body scanning. The percentage of the body you colour is calculated and tracked over time, giving you a direct measure of how your interoceptive awareness is developing across the program.

faq.question: What is the MISS (Mindfulness-based Interoceptive Signature Scale)?
faq.answer: The MISS measures two aspects of body sensations during distressing experiences: Specificity — the particular qualities of sensations, described through four elements: Mass (Earth), Temperature (Fire), Motion (Air), and Fluidity (Water).Intensity — how strong the sensations are. You record sensations both before applying equanimity and after 30 seconds of equanimous observation. The change between the two is your key measure of progress.

faq.question: The MISS slider won't move / I can't save the MISS.
faq.answer: If the slider won't move: Try changing the angle of your finger or using a different finger.Make sure you are moving the dot along the horizontal line, not just tapping it. If the MISS won't save: Make sure all four blue dots on all sliding scales have been moved from their default position.Once the Fluidity dot is moved and the line is drawn, values cannot be changed — tap the back arrow to re-enter if needed.

faq.question: What is the SUDS?
faq.answer: The SUDS (Subjective Units of Distress Scale) measures the level of distress you expect to experience in situations you have been avoiding. You rate five situations on a scale from 0 (no distress) to 100 (maximum distress). Track your progress by re-rating each situation as you work through the Bipolar Exposure technique. As equanimity develops and avoidance decreases, scores should decline over time.

faq.question: How do I choose five situations for the SUDS hierarchy if I have many?
faq.answer: Make a list of 10–12 situations you typically avoid.Choose five that represent an incremental range of distress — from least to most challenging. Example hierarchy: Going out after dark — 20%Speaking with the cashier — 40%Attending a social event — 60%Presenting at work — 80%Confronting a difficult family member — 95% Having a range of difficulty levels means you can start with less challenging situations and work toward harder ones as your equanimity grows.

faq.question: What is the ECD (Ethical Conduct Diary)?
faq.answer: The ECD tracks your effort to apply the five ethical challenges in daily life: Refraining from harm to living beingsRefraining from taking what is not givenRefraining from sexual misconductRefraining from false speechRefraining from intoxicants For each entry, you record the situation, how long you managed to apply effort, and reflect on the experience. The ECD is not a tool of self-judgment — it is a tool of awareness.

faq.question: What is a 'situation of potential harm' in the ECD?
faq.answer: A situation of potential harm is any scenario where your actions could harm yourself or someone else. Examples include: Deceiving othersTaking something that isn't yoursUsing harsh or divisive languageSexually exploiting someoneConsuming intoxicantsAny other behaviour that causes suffering to yourself or others The ECD is not about self-judgment — it is about developing awareness of your patterns and gradually applying more conscious effort.

faq.question: How do I add goals?
faq.answer: Tap Goals in the bottom navigation bar, or navigate to Stage 1 > Step 1 and tap Start.Describe the problem or difficulty you would like to address.Rate how much it affects your life.Tap Save. Try to set 3–5 goals that reflect your main reasons for starting the program. Goals can be specific (e.g., reduce anxiety about social situations) or broader (e.g., feel more settled and in control).

faq.question: How do I edit, update, or mark a goal as complete?
faq.answer: To edit or update a goal: Tap Goals in the bottom navigation bar.Tap the pen icon next to the goal you would like to update.Tick any progress indicators you have achieved.Tap Update to save. To mark a goal as complete: Tap the pen icon next to the goal.Tap Complete and confirm by tapping Goal Achieved? The goal will be moved to your completed list. It is normal for goals to evolve as you progress — revisit and update them at each stage. To delete a goal: Swipe left on the goal and tap the trash icon. Note: deleting is permanent and cannot be undone. If you have achieved a goal, mark it as complete rather than deleting it — that preserves your progress record.

faq.question: The audio isn't playing or keeps stopping.
faq.answer: Work through these steps in order: Check your volume — make sure your device volume is turned up and not set to silent.Check Do Not Disturb — if enabled, it may be silencing the app.Check your internet connection — the audio requires a connection to stream. Connect to Wi-Fi or mobile data.Close and reopen the app completely, then try again.Check for app updates in the App Store or Google Play.Restart your device. If the problem continues, contact support@mindfulness.net.au.

faq.question: The app keeps crashing or won't load.
faq.answer: Try these steps in order: Close and reopen the app completely (force-close).Check for updates in the App Store or Google Play.Restart your device.Uninstall and reinstall the app. Your account data is stored on the server and will not be lost — just log back in after reinstalling. If the problem continues, contact support@mindfulness.net.au with your device type and operating system version.

faq.question: My data isn't saving.
faq.answer: If your practice logs, assessment results, or goals are not saving: Check your internet connection — the app requires an active connection to save data.Check you are logged in — tap More to confirm your account details.Make sure you tap Submit or Save at the end of each entry — data is not auto-saved.Close and reopen the app and check whether the data now appears. If the problem persists, contact support@mindfulness.net.au.

faq.question: I want to stop — the program doesn't seem to be working.
faq.answer: This feeling is very common, and it almost always passes. The question worth sitting with is: what is it that wants to stop? Sometimes a phase occurs where things feel harder before they feel easier — particularly around Stages 1 and 2, when the novelty has worn off and the benefits are not yet obvious. Difficulty and discomfort during practice are often signs that something real is happening, not that you are failing. If something specific is making the program feel unsafe or actively harmful, please contact support or a mental health professional. Otherwise, try returning to your Stage 1.1 baseline assessment scores and comparing them to where you are now — the data often tells a different story than how the practice feels in a hard moment.

faq.question: I missed some sessions / I'm behind in the program. What should I do?
faq.answer: Missing some sessions is normal — life happens. A few guidelines: If you have missed a few days, simply pick up where you left off.If you have missed a week or more, spend a few days reviewing your current stage before moving forward.Do not restart from the beginning unless you feel completely unfamiliar with the practice. The skills you have developed do not disappear with a short break — they may feel rusty initially but return quickly. Consistency matters more than perfection: 13 out of 14 sessions is still excellent progress.

faq.question: I stopped practising for weeks or months. Have I lost all my progress?
faq.answer: No. The skills developed through MiCBT do not simply disappear. They may feel less accessible after a long break, but they return more quickly than they were originally learned — like physical fitness after a break. Simply return to practice, starting with your current stage. If things feel very unfamiliar, step back one stage temporarily. Don't judge yourself for the break — simply begin again.

faq.question: How do I change my password?
faq.answer: Tap More in the bottom navigation bar.Tap Settings.Tap Change Password.Enter your current password, then your new password, and confirm it.Tap Save. This option is only available if you registered with an email address and password. If you signed up with Google, Facebook, or Apple, manage your password through those services.

faq.question: How do I change my email address?
faq.answer: Changing your email address is not currently possible within the app. The only option is to delete your existing account and create a new one — but this will permanently remove all your data including your practice diary, assessment results, and goals. If you need to update your email address, contact support@mindfulness.net.au for advice before taking any action.

faq.question: How do I delete my account or all my data?
faq.answer: To remove data from your device: iPhone: Settings → General → iPhone Storage → MiCBT Guide → Delete App.Android: Settings → Apps → MiCBT Guide → Storage → Clear Data. Important: these steps remove data stored locally on your device. Data stored on the server (your account, diary, assessments, goals) may still be retained. To request full account deletion, contact support@mindfulness.net.au.

faq.question: What data does the app collect?
faq.answer: When you create an account, the MiCBT Guide collects only your: First nameLast nameEmail address Your practice data — including diary entries, assessment results, and goals — is stored securely and handled in accordance with Australian privacy law. Your personal information is not disclosed to third parties for marketing or commercial purposes.

faq.question: How do I share my records with my therapist?
faq.answer: The current version of the MiCBT Guide does not have a built-in feature for directly sharing records with a therapist. In the meantime, you can share your progress by: Taking screenshots of your assessment graphs, diary, or goals.Sending them via email or your preferred platform. If you have a specific request for data export, contact support@mindfulness.net.au — the team may be able to assist.

faq.question: My subscription isn't showing on my new device.
faq.answer: Your subscription is linked to your account, not your device, so it is still valid. Make sure you are logged in to the same account on your new device.If the subscription still does not appear, tap Restore Purchases in the app.If that does not work, contact support@mindfulness.net.au with your account email.

faq.question: I'm not sure mindfulness is for me. I feel guilty sitting down twice a day to meditate when others need my help.
faq.answer: Doubt is a very common theme when beginning this practice. It often falls into three categories: doubt about the technique, doubt about the teacher, or doubt about yourself. The feeling that sitting down to practise is 'selfish' or unproductive is itself a belief worth examining with equanimity. MiCBT is not a passive or self-indulgent practice — it is active training. The skills you develop directly benefit the people around you: reduced reactivity, clearer communication, greater compassion. Continuing to practise despite doubt is itself an act of commitment — and doubting, noticing the doubt, and returning to the practice is the practice.

faq.question: My mind wandered for the entire session and I got nothing done. Should I try again?
faq.answer: Yes — always. There is no such thing as a 'wasted' session. Even a session in which attention wandered continuously still involved moments of noticing the wandering and returning. Those moments — however brief — are genuine practice. The act of noticing that the mind has wandered is the key skill being developed. Do not judge the session by how focused it felt. Judge it by whether you sat, whether you returned when you noticed, and whether you completed the time. Over weeks, the quality of attention deepens — but this happens through consistent practice, including the difficult sessions.

faq.question: I'm in therapy. Can I combine MiCBT with my therapist's approach?
faq.answer: Yes — MiCBT is generally complementary to therapy and many therapists actively recommend it. Inform your therapist that you are using the program so they are aware. If you are working on specific trauma or complex mental health conditions, your therapist can help you calibrate the pace of the program to your therapeutic work. MiCBT does not replace professional mental health care — particularly for severe or complex presentations — but it provides valuable skills and self-monitoring tools that support and extend the benefits of therapy.

faq.question: I practise other meditation traditions (Zen, Vipassana, Transcendental Meditation). Is MiCBT compatible?
faq.answer: MiCBT is compatible with other meditation traditions and does not require any religious or spiritual affiliation. The body scanning technique is closely related to Vipassana (insight meditation) as taught in the Theravada tradition — if you already have a Vipassana practice, you will find much that is familiar. The key difference is MiCBT's explicit integration with CBT techniques (exposure, assertiveness, Bipolar Exposure) and the use of validated assessment tools. Many people with existing meditation practices find that MiCBT deepens and contextualises their practice within a structured therapeutic framework.

faq.question: I'm worried that meditation will change me in ways I don't want.
faq.answer: MiCBT aims to develop specific, well-defined skills: sustained attention, equanimity, reduced automatic reactivity, and compassion. It does not aim to change your personality, values, or identity — it aims to reduce the suffering and unhelpful patterns that prevent you from living in line with your values. Many people describe feeling more like themselves after the program, rather than less. The changes that happen are largely in the direction of the qualities you already value: calm, clarity, kindness, and genuine engagement with life. These changes happen gradually and are under your awareness throughout.

faq.question: I feel like I am a failure at meditation.
faq.answer: Feeling like a failure at meditation is almost universal at some point in practice — and it is itself a valuable object of observation. Notice: where do you feel this in your body? What is the quality of the sensation associated with 'failure'? Can you observe this without reacting to it? The feeling of failure is a thought — a self-evaluative story — and like all thoughts, it arises, exists for a moment, and passes. You cannot fail at meditation by practising. You can only practise well or less well, and both contribute to development. The very fact that you are noticing your experience critically means awareness is alive and working.

faq.question: Can I use the MiCBT app in my research project?
faq.answer: Before using any MiCBT Institute resources or referencing MiCBT in a project, written approval from the MiCBT Institute is required. Please email admin@mindfulness.net.au with details of your project.

faq.question: I have an idea for improving the app.
faq.answer: We love receiving feedback! Please send your idea to support@mindfulness.net.au. Your feedback helps us understand what is working well and what could be improved, and supports the ongoing development of the program.

faq.question: How do I contact support?
faq.answer: For technical issues, account questions, or other enquiries: email support@mindfulness.net.au. Include a description of the issue and your device type (iPhone/Android) and operating system version if reporting a technical problem. For questions about the MiCBT Institute, training, or research: email admin@mindfulness.net.au.

faq.question: I can only see the depression graph in the DASS-21. How do I view the anxiety and stress graphs?
faq.answer: Tap the orange arrow above the graph. Each tap moves you to the next subscale graph. The DASS-21 tracks all three scales separately — Depression, Anxiety, and Stress — each with its own trend graph.

faq.question: How do I use the ES-16?
faq.answer: Tap Assessments and select ES-16.Read each statement and select a response indicating how much it applied to you over the past week.Answer with your first instinct — don't spend too long on any item.Tap Submit. There are no right or wrong answers. Complete it honestly — it is a tool for your own tracking and insight.

faq.question: Should I focus on the Experiential Acceptance or Non-Reactivity subscale of the ES-16, or the total score?
faq.answer: Focus on the total ES-16 score. Research by Dr Alice Shires, Dr Bruno Cayoun, and the MiCBT research team shows that equanimity is a combination of both acceptance and non-reactivity, so the total score is the most accurate measure. The percentile score is also useful — it shows how your result compares with the general population. Individual subscale scores can give useful clues — for example, if non-reactivity is significantly lower than acceptance, it may indicate you are allowing sensations to be present but still acting on them — but treat these as supplementary rather than primary measures.

faq.question: How do I use the MSES-R?
faq.answer: Tap Assessments and select MSES-R.Read each statement and select a response indicating how much you agree with it today.Answer based on how confident you feel right now — not how you think you should feel.Tap Submit. There are no right or wrong answers. Do not spend too much time on any statement.

faq.question: I can only see the Emotion Regulation subscale graph in the MSES-R. Are there graphs for the other subscales?
faq.answer: Yes — the MSES-R has six subscales, each with its own graph. To navigate between them, tap the orange right arrow above the graph. To go back, tap the left arrow. The six subscales are: Emotion Regulation, Equanimity, Social Skills, Distress Tolerance, Taking Responsibility, and Interpersonal Effectiveness.

faq.question: What are the six MSES-R subscales and what does each measure?
faq.answer: The MSES-R measures confidence in applying mindfulness skills across six domains: Emotion Regulation — managing intense emotions without being overwhelmed.Equanimity — observing experiences without reactivity.Social Skills — engaging effectively and mindfully in social situations.Distress Tolerance — tolerating difficult experiences without avoidance or reactive coping.Taking Responsibility — recognising and owning the impact of your actions.Interpersonal Effectiveness — communicating and relating to others in ways that reflect your values. The total score is the most direct measure of overall mindfulness-based self-efficacy.

faq.question: What are 'Community Norms' in the MSES-R?
faq.answer: Community Norms are the scores of the general population used as a benchmark to compare your own MSES-R results. They indicate how people without severe mental health conditions typically score on each subscale. The norms were derived from data collected from over 5,000 people internationally — primarily from Australia, Canada, New Zealand, the USA, and the UK. Seeing your score relative to these norms gives a practical sense of how your confidence in applying mindfulness compares to a broad, healthy population.

faq.question: Which MSES-R result should I focus on most?
faq.answer: Focus on the total MSES-R score — it is the most direct measure of mindfulness-based self-efficacy and the primary metric to track across the program. The percentile score is also useful, as it shows how your result compares with the broader population. Individual subscale scores can reveal specific strengths and growth areas. For example, high Equanimity but lower Social Skills may indicate the formal meditation practice is progressing well while interpersonal application is still developing.

faq.question: How do I use the SWLS?
faq.answer: Tap Assessments and select SWLS.Read each statement and select a response indicating how much you agree with it today.Avoid comparing your life to others — base your answers on your own internal experience.Tap Submit. There are no right or wrong answers. Answer honestly from your genuine felt sense rather than how you think you should feel.

faq.question: How is my SWLS life satisfaction score calculated?
faq.answer: Your SWLS score is determined by comparing your answers to a large sample of people from diverse backgrounds. The scale produces a total score from 5 to 35, ranging from Extremely Dissatisfied to Extremely Satisfied relative to population norms. The app tracks your score over time so you can see how your sense of life satisfaction changes as you progress through the program.

faq.question: My SWLS result doesn't seem accurate. Why?
faq.answer: The SWLS is very sensitive to how you answer. For an accurate result: Answer each question honestly, without comparing your life to others — either those better off or worse off than you.Base your answers on your own genuine experience of your life as it is, not how you think it should be.Answer for how you genuinely feel today, not how you felt in the past or hope to feel in the future. Even small shifts in framing can significantly change your score, so consistency in how you approach the questions matters for meaningful trend tracking.

faq.question: How do I use the IAI?
faq.answer: After completing a body scanning session, tap Assessments and select IAI.Colour in all areas of the body shape where you felt any sensations during your practice — even very subtle sensations count.To make colouring faster, increase the brush size by sliding the brush tool (left side of the screen) to the right.When finished, tap Save. Be as accurate as possible. The app calculates the percentage of the body coloured and tracks this over time.

faq.question: Can I undo a stroke in the IAI body map?
faq.answer: The current version of the app does not have an undo function for individual strokes. However, you can clear all colouring and start again by tapping Clear. Tip: adjust the brush size before you begin — larger brushes cover more area with each stroke, making colouring faster and reducing the need for corrections.

faq.question: Colouring the IAI body shape takes too long. Can I make the brush bigger?
faq.answer: Yes. Use the brush tool on the left-hand side of the body shape and slide the dot toward the right to increase the brush size. A larger brush covers more area per stroke, making colouring significantly faster. Tap Clear if you want to start over with a new brush size.

faq.question: Some of my past IAI dates have disappeared from the graph. Where did they go?
faq.answer: To keep the graph readable over time, the IAI graph displays only the first and last 6 measurements. All your results are still stored — scroll down below the graph to see a full list of every entry. You can delete any entry by swiping it to the left and tapping the trash icon. Note: if you delete an entry from the list, it will no longer appear on the graph either.

faq.question: How do I use the MISS?
faq.answer: After or during an intense experience, tap Assessments and select MISS.Using the four sliding scales (Mass, Temperature, Motion, Fluidity), rate the qualities of the body sensations you felt.Record your pre-equanimity rating — how the sensation was at its most intense.Apply equanimity to those sensations for about 30 seconds.Record your post-equanimity rating.Make sure all four blue dots have been moved before tapping Save. The MISS will not save if any scale remains at its default position.

faq.question: I can't see the Specificity indicators in the MISS. What are they?
faq.answer: The Specificity indicators appear when you tap or move the dot on a horizontal sliding scale — they do not display as static labels.
